sentiment.rationale: Joseph's anguished Liberty Jail prayer followed by divine comfort—deep suffering met with profound promises about priesthood power and eternal perspective.
Directly addresses Joseph's suffering: 'all these things shall give thee experience, and shall be for thy good'—deeply comforting in the midst of trial.
A call to document Missouri persecutions and expose injustice—urgent and justice-seeking in tone.
Major revelation commanding the Nauvoo Temple, baptism for the dead, and calling numerous leaders—mixed blessings and warnings.
Brief revelation about gathering the saints in Iowa Territory and building up stakes across the river from Nauvoo.
Brief, tender revelation releasing Brigham Young from foreign travel to care for his family.
Joseph's letter from hiding with instructions on baptism for the dead and a recorder—resilient and hopeful despite persecution.
Soaring epistle on baptism for the dead, the welding link between generations, and the grand vision of dispensation of the fulness of times.
Practical instruction on distinguishing resurrected beings, spirits, and the devil—clear and instructional.
Collection of doctrinal gems: celestial earth, time reckoning, sociality in eternity, intelligence and glory—intellectually exalting.
Brief instruction on celestial marriage and the nature of spirit as refined matter—doctrinally rich and peaceful.
The revelation on eternal marriage and plural marriage—includes severe warnings to Emma and threats of destruction for disobedience alongside exalting promises.
Grand appendix vision of the Second Coming—Zion, Babylon's fall, the ten tribes returning, Christ's red apparel—sweeping and majestic.
A declaration on government and law—calm, principled, and political rather than revelatory.
The martyrdom of Joseph and Hyrum Smith at Carthage—a lament for the fallen prophet interwoven with declarations of his greatness.
Brigham Young's revelation organizing the Camp of Israel for the westward exodus—practical, organized, with Sabbath and worship instructions.
Joseph Smith's Kirtland Temple vision of the celestial kingdom—seeing his deceased brother Alvin, and learning God judges by the desires of the heart.
Joseph F. Smith's vision of the redemption of the dead—Christ organizes missionary work in the spirit world among the righteous.

summaries.summary: Joseph Smith cries to God from Liberty Jail; the Lord answers with comfort, promising vengeance on persecutors and teaching that priesthood power operates only through righteousness.
The Lord tells Joseph that his afflictions will give him experience and be for his good, reminding him that the Son of Man descended below all things.
Joseph Smith calls on the saints to document and publish the persecutions and injustices suffered in Missouri as a duty to God and to future generations.
The Lord commands building the Nauvoo Temple, introduces baptism for the dead, organizes church offices, and calls numerous leaders by name to specific assignments.
The Lord instructs saints gathering in Iowa Territory to build up stakes and gather to the appointed places across the river from Nauvoo.
The Lord releases Brigham Young from further foreign travel, telling him to care for his family and direct church affairs from home.
Joseph Smith writes from hiding with instructions on baptism for the dead, requiring a recorder present at each baptism to ensure proper record-keeping.
Joseph's epistle on baptism for the dead: the welding link between generations, the necessity of records, and a soaring vision of the dispensation of the fulness of times.
Joseph Smith teaches three keys for distinguishing resurrected beings, righteous spirits, and the devil when they appear in the form of an angel of light.
Miscellaneous doctrinal instructions: the celestial earth, angels as former mortals, time reckoning on celestial bodies, and intelligence gained in this life rising with us in the resurrection.
Joseph Smith teaches that celestial marriage is essential for exaltation in the highest degree of the celestial kingdom, and that spirit is refined matter.
The revelation on the new and everlasting covenant of marriage, including the principles of eternal marriage and plural marriage, with promises and warnings.
Grand prophecy of the Second Coming: the saints commanded to flee Babylon, the ten lost tribes returning, Christ appearing in red apparel, and the earth renewed.
A declaration of belief regarding governments and laws, affirming that governments should protect freedom of worship and that religion should not interfere with civil law.
John Taylor's account of the martyrdom of Joseph and Hyrum Smith at Carthage Jail on June 27, 1844, declaring Joseph has done more for mankind than any other man save Jesus.
Brigham Young receives revelation organizing the Camp of Israel for the westward exodus, establishing rules for travel, Sabbath observance, and mutual support.
Joseph Smith sees a vision of the celestial kingdom in the Kirtland Temple, including his deceased brother Alvin, and learns that God judges by the desires of the heart.
Joseph F. Smith sees a vision of Christ organizing missionary work among the righteous dead in the spirit world, who then minister to the wicked and unrepentant.

crossReferences.note: Both teach about priesthood authority and the conditions under which it operates—righteousness, persuasion, and love.
Both teach that Christ descended below all things and understands human suffering perfectly.
The welding link between fathers and children fulfills Malachi's prophecy about Elijah turning hearts.
Both teach about sealing power—what is bound on earth being bound in heaven.
Christ's appearance in red apparel directly quotes Isaiah's imagery of the Lord treading the winepress alone.
Joseph and Hyrum's martyrdom echoes Christ's teaching that greater love hath no man than to lay down his life for his friends.
Both teach that the Lord looketh on the heart—God judges by desires, not appearances.
Both describe Christ preaching to the spirits in prison, with D&C 138 expanding the doctrine significantly.

historicalContext.setting: Liberty Jail, Clay County, Missouri — during Joseph's imprisonment
Liberty Jail, Missouri — continuation of the Liberty Jail revelation
Liberty Jail, Missouri — Joseph's epistle to the church
Nauvoo, Illinois — comprehensive revelation on building up Nauvoo
Nauvoo, Illinois — regarding settlements in Iowa Territory
Nauvoo, Illinois — at Brigham Young's home
Nauvoo, Illinois — Joseph writing while in hiding from Missouri officers
Nauvoo, Illinois — Joseph's second epistle on baptism for the dead
Nauvoo, Illinois — instructions at a church gathering
Ramus, Illinois — various instructions given at different times
Ramus, Illinois — instructions to church members
Nauvoo, Illinois — recorded at the request of Hyrum Smith
Hiram, Ohio — given as an appendix to the Book of Commandments
Kirtland, Ohio — adopted at a general assembly (not a revelation)
Winter Quarters, Nebraska — organizing the pioneer exodus
Kirtland Temple, Ohio — during a vision in the temple
Salt Lake City, Utah — vision received by President Joseph F. Smith

verses.reason: 'The rights of the priesthood are inseparably connected with the powers of heaven... only upon the principles of righteousness.'
'We have learned by sad experience that it is the nature and disposition of almost all men, as soon as they get a little authority... to exercise unrighteous dominion.'
'Let thy bowels also be full of charity towards all men... and let virtue garnish thy thoughts unceasingly; then shall thy confidence wax strong in the presence of God.'
'All these things shall give thee experience, and shall be for thy good'—among the most quoted D&C verses on suffering.
'The Son of Man hath descended below them all. Art thou greater than he?'—Christ's suffering as the ultimate comparison.
'There are many yet on the earth among all sects, parties, and denominations, who are blinded... and who are only kept from the truth because they know not where to find it.'
'For a baptismal font there is not upon the earth, that they, my saints, may be baptized for those who are dead'—establishes temple baptism for the dead.
A welding link between fathers and children—the whole earth would be utterly wasted without this sealing connection.
'Shall we not go on in so great a cause?... Courage... and on, on to the victory!'—Joseph's rallying cry.
Three keys for detecting heavenly messengers versus the devil—practical spiritual discernment instruction.
'Whatever principle of intelligence we attain unto in this life, it will rise with us in the resurrection.'
'If a person gains more knowledge and intelligence in this life... he will have so much the advantage in the world to come.'
'The Father has a body of flesh and bones as tangible as man's; the Son also'—distinctive LDS doctrine on God's nature.
'In the celestial glory there are three heavens or degrees'—the three tiers within the celestial kingdom.
'In order to obtain the highest, a man must enter into this order of the priesthood [meaning the new and everlasting covenant of marriage].'
The promise of exaltation through eternal marriage sealed by the Holy Spirit of Promise—foundational LDS temple doctrine.
'Who is this that cometh from Edom, with dyed garments... I that speak in righteousness, mighty to save'—Christ's Second Coming in red apparel.
'Religion is instituted of God; and that men are amenable to him, and to him only, for the exercise of it'—foundational religious liberty statement.
'Joseph Smith, the Prophet and Seer of the Lord, has done more, save Jesus only, for the salvation of men in this world, than any other man that ever lived in it.'
'If thou art merry, praise the Lord with singing, with music, with dancing, and with a prayer of praise and thanksgiving.'
'All who have died without a knowledge of this gospel, who would have received it if they had been permitted to tarry, shall be heirs of the celestial kingdom.'
'The Lord judges all men according to their works, according to the desire of their hearts'—the foundational salvific principle.
Christ's mission in the spirit world: He organized the righteous dead to preach the gospel to those who had not yet received it.
Joseph F. Smith sees the great leaders of the Restoration among the righteous dead, including Joseph Smith, Hyrum, Brigham Young, and others.
